content.problemStatement:
Two players (let's call them A and B) are playing a game on a row of cells. A cell may contain the character 'A', contain the character 'B' or be empty. Each character may only be moved from a cell cic_ici​ to a cell cfc_fcf​ if cell cfc_fcf​ and all cells between cic_ici​ and cfc_fcf​ are currently empty. The leftmost (first) character may only be moved to the right, the second character may only be moved to the left, the next character to the right, and so on. Each character may be moved any number of times, including zero.

The players alternate turns; player A starts. On each turn, the current player must choose a cell containing their own character ('A' for player A, 'B' for player B) and move it to a different cell. This cell may be chosen arbitrarily as long as all rules described above are satisfied. The first player that cannot move a character loses.

Note that each character is always moved in a fixed direction, so the game is finite. Determine the winner of the game if both players play optimally.
constraints: The sum of |s| over all test cases does not exceed 10^6
content.inputFormat: The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows. The first and only line of each test case contains a single string sss describing the initial state of the row of cells. Each character of sss is either 'A', 'B' or '.', denoting a cell containing 'A', a cell containing 'B' or an empty cell respectively.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single line containing the string "A" if player A wins or "B" if player B wins.

sampleTestCases.explanation: In the first test case, player A can move the 'A' to the right, and then player B cannot move, so player A wins. In the second test case, player A can move the 'A' two cells to the right, and then player B cannot move, so player A wins. In the third test case, player A and player B can move their 'A's and 'B's respectively, but in the end, player A will be the one who cannot move, so player B wins.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use a simple game theory approach. We will count the number of 'A's and 'B's in the string and their positions. Then, we will determine the winner based on the number of 'A's and 'B's and their positions.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * |s|) — where T is the number of test cases and |s| is the length of the string in each test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(T * |s|) — where T is the number of test cases and |s| is the length of the string in each test case.
